coredata.dc:title: New strategy of sustainable development: Interface process exploration between Mikania-derived carbon dots and sesame growth
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shuting
Chen, Junjie
Zhang, Qilei
Liu, Xingyu
Wang, Aiqi
Sun, Wenjie
Huang, Jundong
Peng, Changlian
Wang, Qianming
coredata.dc:description: Mikania, a well-accepted invasive plant in nature, has been difficult to be completely removed even based on chemical herbicides. Moreover, the employment of chemical herbicides will lead to hazardous results to health or environment. In this study, the whole-new carbon polymer quantum dots (CPDs) have been afforded in the light of Mikania as the carbon source. Moreover, sesame has been selected as a plant model and the effect of such kind of nanoscale fertilizer on its growth has been explored. Results support that CPDs at a concentration of 0–0.4 mg/mL possess a physiological response to the germination and no toxicity has been observed. Confocal images demonstrate that CPDs can spread through the roots of sesame seeds to the stems and leaves. The utilization of micrantha-derived carbon quantum dots with fluorescence characteristics in the growth of sesame will give rise to new ideas for waste-plant management and biological control, and strengthen the importance of nanomaterials in related fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Micrantha
Fluorescence
Interface

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure characteristics of CPDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Q. M. appreciates Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (No. 2021A1515010324 ) and Science and Technology Plan of Guangdong Province (No. 2020A0505100055 ). A.Q. thanks for the General Colleges Annual Youth Innovative Talents Project of Guangdong Province ( 2021KQNCX224 ). Q.M. also gratefully acknowledges the support of 2022 Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area Exchange Programs of SCNU and also thanks for innovation and entrepreneurship team project of Shunde district in Foshan city (No. 2230218002015), Guangdong Province. The authors thank for the useful discussion by Professor Cheng Zhang from University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center at Dallas. W.J. thanks for Special Funds for the Cultivation of Guangdong College Students' Scientific and Technological Innovation (“Climbing Program”Special Funds 2023).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CPDs. (Inset Figure: HRTEM images of CPDs). (b) Particle size distribution of CPDs.
XPS survey and deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of carbon polymer quantum dots for C1s, N1s and O1s.

(a). UV–Visible absorption spectrum of CPDs. (b). Photoluminescence spectra of CPDs (Inset Figure: Photographs of the aqueous solution of CPDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light irradiation (right)).
Captured pictures of sesame seedlings treated with CPDs solution for 7 days. CPDs solution with concentration of 0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4 mg/mL, respectively (from left to right).

Effect of CPDs on the growth and development of sesame seedlings after incubation for 7 days. (a) Length of root, (b) Length of stem, (c) Fresh weight and (d) Moisture level of sesame seedlings grown on different concentrations of CPDs. Error bars represent standard deviation.

Laser Scanning Confocal Microscopy(LSCM) images of transverse sections from root, stem and vertical sections from leaf in sesame, cultivated with CPDs solution (0.4 mg/mL) for 5 days. All images were captured under the same exposure conditions.
Schematic representation for the CPDs synthesis and plant culture.

head.ce:title: New strategy of sustainable development: Interface process exploration between Mikania-derived carbon dots and sesame growth

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mikania, a well-accepted invasive plant in nature, has been difficult to be completely removed even based on chemical herbicides. Moreover, the employment of chemical herbicides will lead to hazardous results to health or environment. In this study, the whole-new carbon polymer quantum dots (CPDs) have been afforded in the light of Mikania as the carbon source. Moreover, sesame has been selected as a plant model and the effect of such kind of nanoscale fertilizer on its growth has been explored. Results support that CPDs at a concentration of 0–0.4 mg/mL possess a physiological response to the germination and no toxicity has been observed. Confocal images demonstrate that CPDs can spread through the roots of sesame seeds to the stems and leaves. The utilization of micrantha-derived carbon quantum dots with fluorescence characteristics in the growth of sesame will give rise to new ideas for waste-plant management and biological control, and strengthen the importance of nanomaterials in related fields.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Compositae has been considered as the most valuable plant families in the world, but it also contains the huge number of invasive alien species [1,2]. For example, Mikania grows extremely fast and the speed can reach as fast as 20 cm per day [3]. Panicles terminal or lateral and related species, which are generated from South and Central America, have been exported to almost all over the world [4]. The invasion of Mikania micrantha has been closely related to gene loss and species diversity reduction, especially caused economic problems in local forest and crop production [5]. Such plant species would occupy the largest proportion of sunlight irradiation and result in full growth in contrast to other crops. The typical allelopathy effect has been observed in the biological systems [6]. Researches confirmed that Mikania micrantha severely changed the soil properties through the release of sesquiterpene lactones (STLs) and phenolic compounds, thus suppressing the growth of nearby plants [7,8]. The conventional treatment of controlling Mikania micrantha is to choose the herbicides and other chemicals. No effective ways and approaches to decrease the negative influence of the plant have been reported.

Carbon nanoscale materials, including carbon dots (CDs), polymer dots (PDs) and carbon polymer dots (CPDs), have been widely exploited in recent years due to their advantages of eco-friendliness, biocompatibility and high stability. These particles have quasi-spherical microstructures and internal carbonaceous core. The average diameter could be adjusted to 1–10 nm [9]. CDs possess a higher degree of carbonization structure, PDs have a coverage of polymer-based surface, and CPDs included the above-mentioned features, which are composed of organic building blocks from outside and the inorganic carbon core [10]. The functional materials can be synthesized by arc discharge method [11], microwave synthesis [12], chemical oxidation [13], combustion [14] and hydrothermal synthesis [15,16]. It can be well documented that the carbon-based nanoplatforms exhibit unique and distinguished luminescent signals. The most dramatic aspect of fluorescence would be its occurrence at wavelength longer than that of absorption curve. A valuable property of such nanoparticle would be its sensitivity to the surrounding environment and it make fluorescent band to be most effective to show the chemical recognition process. Such characterization way (fluorescence) will be free from using analyte standard and no reference sample is included. The signal can be monitored at long distance and will be favorable for remote sensing. As for the carbon quantum dots, the most attractive property would be its capability to modulate wavelength across the entire visible spectrum. The emission performance could contribute to the application in biological imaging [17] (Scheme 1 ).

As the main role in the ecosystem, plants exhibit the key function of energy and mass transportation in the biological system via absorptive behavior and bioaccumulation [18]. Nanomaterials could be adsorbed from the environment and they might transfer through the plant. The interaction of nanoscale particles with plants has also been focused, including carbon nanotubes [19], fullerenes [20] and metal oxides [21]. Since the tiny particles are incorporated into the plant through the air or the root system, the exact beneficial or harmful role to the plant should be identified. Therefore, how to use the nanomaterials with luminescence in botany requires further studies.

In this study, the harmful and invasive plant Mikania micrantha in common sense was employed as the carbon source, and the new CPDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method assisted by acid treatment. The nanoparticles represent blue emission in the aqueous solution and can remain stable for a long time. Most of cation/anions have trivial impact on the nanostructure, which make it possible to be used in plants. Sesame seeds were selected as plant carrier to study the dose effects of different concentrations on growth process. In addition, the transportation and distribution of CPDs after germination were also considered by in situ observation. The transformation from waste species into functional material would pave new way for the development of green chemistry and control the overuse of chemical herbicides.

Mikania was randomly selected from the university campus of local area. All chemicals were commercially available and used without further purification. Nitric acid and hydrogen peroxide (30% H2O2) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by a Hitachi FL4600 spectrophotometer. A quartz cuvette with the path-length of 1 cm has been employed to obtain the fluorescent signals. Both the excitation and emission slit width has been fixed at 5 nm. The scanning speed for the sample measurement has been maintained at 1200 nm/min with the voltage at 700 V. The quantum efficiency for the sample has been evaluated by an integrating sphere (Edinburgh FLS 920 spectrometer). Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra were collected by a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrophotometer. The microstructures were recorded by a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL JEM-2100F). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured within the 4000–400 cm−1 region on an infrared spectrophotometer, Prestingge- 21, using the KBr pellet technique. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) data were collected on a Bruker D8-ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a VG ESCALAB 220iXL surface analysis system. Zeta potential of CPDs was determined by Malvern Nano-ZS90 system (Malvern, United Kingdom). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) experiments were carried out using SII EXStar6000 TG/DTA6300 thermal analyzer from room temperature to 800 °C under an air atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1. Laser scanning microscopy (LSM) was performed with a LSM 710, Zeiss, equipped with 405 nm laser for excitation. NMR spectra were recorded with a Bruker AVANCE NEO in D2O solvent. Chemical shifts (δ) are reported in parts per million (ppm). Gel permeation chromatography was performed with an Agilent PL-GPC50/Agilent 1260.

The CPDs were effectively synthesized via a hydrothermal method. Briefly, the Mikania leaves was washed three times with ultrapure water. After drying at 80 °C for 2 days, the dry Mikania leaves was ground into powder, 10 g powder were mixed with 90 ml nitric acid (0.1 M). Then, the mixed solution was refluxed at 110 °C for 24 h. The reacted solution was transferred into a 150 mL hydrothermal reaction kettle and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After the temperature was cooled down to room temperature, the resulting CPDs solution was placed in an ultrasonic bath for 20 min. Then it was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min to remove the large particles. The supernatant was filtered through a membrane (0.22 µm) to remove the small amount of precipitation, and then dialyzed with dialysis membranes (Molecular Weight Cut Off (MWCO) = 1000 Da) by ultrapure water for 48 h. Finally, the resulting solution was lyophilized under vacuum for 24 h to give brown solid CPDs with the yield of 6.5% for further analysis and characterization.

Sesame was selected with similar size and then sterilized by treating with 10% NaClO solution for 10 min. Afterwards, seeds were washed with deionized water for three times to remove the residue and then put into 100 ml beakers (10 seeds each group). CPDs solution at different concentrations (0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4 mg/ml) was added into the above beakers, and then covered and transferred into a growth chamber at 25 °C without the light. After 7 days, the sesame seed sprouts were recorded. The experiment was repeated for three times. Calculation of the root length and stem length and fresh weight was performed. The experimental data were analyzed by one-way ANOVA to investigate the statistical differences. P values below 0.05 or 0.01 are denoted as significantly statistical different.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characteristics of CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of CPDs was verified by transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CPDs were regular in spherical shape. The particle size was measured to be from 1.75 nm to 3.65 nm (Fig. 1b). The average diameter was around 2.9 nm. High resolution image (the inset in Fig. 1a) indicated that the CPDs had high crystallinity, with a crystal plane spacing of 0.22 nm. Moreover, the XRD pattern (Fig. S1) of CPDs gave a wide peak at 23°, and the diffraction peak (002) corresponding to the graphite structure has a layer spacing of 0.39 nm [22]. This value was slightly higher than that of graphite (0.34 nm), indicating the presence of a large number of groups on the surface [23]. In the curve of Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S2), graphite band (G band) was located at 1563 cm−1 and it was referred as the core structure. D Band is the representation of sp2 hybrid carbon atoms and the signal was found at 1320 cm−1. The intensity ratio ID/IG was calculated to be 0.44, demonstrating the ordered structure of the crystal is relatively high. The above evidence indicated that CPDs could be covered by organic layer with functional groups and the internal core was composed of orderly arranged graphite carbon atoms.

Although the particle size of CPDs has been given for a long time, its internal structure and atomic distribution density have not been clearly studied. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) can separate substances with different relative molecular weights to study the average relative molecular weight. As can be seen from Fig. S3, a relatively narrow material peak appears from 16 min, with a weight-average molecular weight of 1976 g/mol and PD = 1.26 (Table 1 ). The results revealed that the molecular weight distribution of CPDs could be relatively homogeneous. The inverted signal followed by the material peak was derived from the solvent, which might be caused by the solution influence or injection into the bubble during measurement.

At the same time, 1H-NMR and 13C-NMR spectra of CPDs can also verify that the CPDs surface was covered by a large number of chain aliphatic hydrocarbons. The 1H-NMR spectra were shown in Fig. S4, and the signal in the range δ = 0.5- 1.75 ppm was ascribed to be sp3 hybridized alkyl protons. The characteristic peak at δ = 2.12 ppm could be assigned to alkyl chains attached to CO groups. Additionally, the peak at δ = 3.57 ppm was attributed to alkyl group attached to N/O. In general, the above protons were corresponding to sp3 hybrid proton peaks, while sp2 and sp3 hybrid proton peaks were distributed in the range δ = 4.8 - 8 ppm. The carbon core could hardly contact with the solvent because it was fully wrapped with low molecular weight polymer. In 13C-NMR spectra (Fig. S5), the samples peaks in the range δ = 179–184 ppm may represent the carboxyl groups. On the other hand, all the peaks for the alkyl chains (-CH3; -CH2; -O-CH2; -CH2-NO2) were observed in the range δ = 10 - 75 ppm. In this sense, carbon polymer quantum dots contained aliphatic hydrocarbons on the surface. But the nanoparticle was not the general concept of single molecule, so 1H-NMR and 13C-NMR spectra have difficulty in accurately analyzing its specific components or structure.

In the field of nano-chemistry, functional groups generally determine the reactivity and substance properties. X-ray energy spectrum can be used to check the surface feature of CPDs. In Fig. 2 , the evaluation results showed that the nanostructure is mainly composed of three elements (C, N and O). The resolved C1s spectra indicated the presence of C-C, C-O, C=O/C=N groups, located at 284 eV, 287 eV and 288.8 eV, respectively. In O1s curves, the deconvoluted peaks were contributed by C–O (531.6 eV) and C = O (532.7 eV) units. The high-resolution N1s spectra included the signals of N-H (401.6 eV) and pyrrolic N (399.9 eV).

Infrared spectra further supported the presence of functional groups (Fig. 3 ). The band at 3331 cm−1 was caused by the vibrations of oxygen-containing groups (-OH). The signal at around 2800 cm−1 would be derived from the stretching vibrations of methylene groups. The C=O stretch band at 1652 cm−1 was related to the presence of hydroxyl functional groups. The overlapping bands at 1608 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of C=C and C=N bonds. The peak at 1401 cm−1 was assigned to the absorption band of C-H stretching. The unique band at 1128 cm−1 was referred to the vibrations of C-N.

Fig. S6 represented the thermogravimetric analysis diagram of the CPDs. The initial mass loss (16 wt%) was due to the evaporation of surface absorbed water between 0 °C and 150 °C. In temperature range of 150 °C-350 °C, a weight loss of 34 wt% was found, which might confirm the decarboxylation process of the carboxylic group and deamination behavior of nitrogen moiety [24]. At elevated temperature (350 °C– 500 °C) removal of amorphous carbon on the CPDs surface and bonding with -OH groups took place, resulting in a quality decrease of 13 wt% [24]. At temperature higher than 500 °C, the weight loss would be derived from the degradation of the remaining disordered carbon. The average zeta potential measurement of CPDs was measured to be −18.2 mV (Fig. S7). The high negative charge substantiated that the surface was densely distributed by a variety of carboxyl groups.

The UV–vis and emission spectra were collected to investigate the optical properties of the functional nanodomains [25,26]. Fig. 4 a clearly indicated that CPDs solution had strong absorption in UV and visible light region. Two optical absorption peaks were located at 261 nm and 367 nm. According to the principles of carbon dots, the signals were correlated to π-π* transition of sp2 carbons and n-π* transition of C = O bands. The peak wavelength of the excitation band was found at 352 nm and the emission peak was situated at 450 nm (Fig. 4b). A striking blue emission was achieved. References verified that nanoparticles less than 4 nm would give rise to the luminescence in such range [27–29]. Following exposure to the irradiation light, fluorescence has been related to the nature of excited state. The electron in the excited orbital would be paired to the second electron in the ground state. Consequently, the spin could be allowed and the effect induced the emission of photons. The emission curve was the plot of intensity versus wavelength and the emitting signals were specific to the excitation energy (π-π* transition or n-π* transition). The inset figure in Fig. 4b clearly demonstrated the emission feature under irradiation by visible light and UV light. In addition, the nanostructure exhibited excellent water solubility and would be rather promising in the application of environmental detection or biological sensing. In Fig. S8, the emission band was gradually shifted to longer wavelength when the excitation wavelength was moved from 302 to 422 nm, a typical excitation-dependence effect was obtained. The red shift has been observed and the excitation-dependent effect indicated the changes would be contributed by quantum size effect or surface states. We could find evidence from TEM images and the particle distribution was somewhat irregular. It might also be caused by the existence of different emissive species in the nanodomains. The combination of internal core and outside structure would give rise to the curve evolution results. In XPS data, it has been found that various forms of C, O and N including C-C, C-O, C=O/C=N, NH or pyrrolic N would lead to diverse trapping states with differential energy levels.

In Fig. S9, the pH-correlation versus photoluminescence was recorded. Under strong acidic and alkaline conditions, the fluorescence intensity would be suppressed. In the relatively broad range from pH = 4 to 12, the blue signal could be maintained. As provided in Fig. S10, the fluorescence lifetime of carbon polymer quantum dots was determined to be 2.8 ns. The afforded quantum yield was calculated to be around 6.0% (Table S1). It has to be mentioned that the achieved efficiency for such nanoparticle was relatively limited. The quantum yield is the number of emitted photons relative to the quantities of absorbed photons and the value is relevant to the emissive rate and non-radiative decay. In the above XPS and NMR analysis, the nanomaterial contained a variety of methylene groups (CH2) and hydroxyl moieties (OH). Such units possessed high-frequency vibrations or strong interaction with solvents and they might increase the probability of non-irradiative transitions which would be detrimental to suppress the emitted signals. Ionic interference experiments were carried out in Fig. S11. The results showed that the common ions rarely affect the luminescence behavior of CPDs, it would be possible that the nanomaterials could play critical roles in plant growth.

To visualize the distribution of carbon polymer quantum dots in sesame bud, Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope images were captured in Fig. 7 . The uptake of CPDs can be clearly seen due to the release of strong blue luminescent emission under 409 nm excitation. The bright-field images of roots, stems and leaves were studied and superimposed with fluorescence imaging. In contrast, no CPDs luminescence was detected in the control group treated with pure water (Fig. S12). Through the image analysis, the facts verified that the nanoparticles could enter the plant system and come across the cell membranes. However, in the process of movement from root, stem to leaf, the concentration of CPDs was decreased, indicating that the transporting distance would be primarily important in the distribution of the particles.

The bio-waste could be utilized for sustainable development in carbon dots preparation and more carbon/nitrogen containing compounds could be discovered. Herein, the fluorescent carbon polymer quantum dots (CPDs) were synthesized by using Mikania. The mutual interaction with sesame seeds, including the dose effect of CPDs on the germination and growth rate, has been explored. The adsorption and distribution were recorded. Concentration of CPDs in sesame seedlings with 0–0.4 mg/ mL had effects on the fresh weight, dry weight, stem and root of sesame seeds. These results will she new lights on the full studies concerning precursor sources selection strategy for the assembly of functional nanomaterials.

Shuting Zhang: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Junjie Chen: Investigation. Qilei Zhang: Validation. Xingyu Liu: Resources. Aiqi Wang: Writing – review & editing. Wenjie Sun: Software. Jundong Huang: Investigation. Changlian Peng: Methodology. Qianming Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Q. M. appreciates Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (No. 2021A1515010324) and Science and Technology Plan of Guangdong Province (No. 2020A0505100055). A.Q. thanks for the General Colleges Annual Youth Innovative Talents Project of Guangdong Province (2021KQNCX224). Q.M. also gratefully acknowledges the support of 2022 Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area Exchange Programs of SCNU and also thanks for innovation and entrepreneurship team project of Shunde district in Foshan city (No. 2230218002015), Guangdong Province. The authors thank for the useful discussion by Professor Cheng Zhang from University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center at Dallas. W.J. thanks for Special Funds for the Cultivation of Guangdong College Students' Scientific and Technological Innovation (“Climbing Program”Special Funds 2023).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2022.102469.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Predicting the invasive trend of exotic plants in China based on the ensemble model under climate change: a case for three invasive plants of Asteraceae

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Fang, X. Zhang, H. Wei, D. Wang, R. Chen, L. Wang, W. Gu, Predicting the invasive trend of exotic plants in China based on the ensemble model under climate change: a case for three invasive plants of Asteraceae, Sci. Total Environ. 756 (2021) 143841.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Global biodiversity: indicators of recent declines

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.H. Butchart, M. Walpole, B. Collen, A. Van Strien, J.P.W. Scharlemann, R.E. Almond, J.E. Baillie, B. Bomhard, C. Brown, J. Bruno, Global biodiversity: indicators of recent declines, Science 328 (5982) (2010) 1164–1168.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mikania micrantha genome provides insights into the molecular mechanism of rapid growth

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biology and impacts of pacific islands invasive species. 13. Mikania micrantha Kunth (Asteraceae)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.D. Day, D.R. Clements, C. Gile, W.K. Senaratne, S. Shen, L.A. Weston, F. Zhang, Biology and impacts of pacific islands invasive species. 13. Mikania micrantha Kunth (Asteraceae), Pac. Sci. 70 (3) (2016) 257–285.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaluation of rotary drum composting for the management of invasive weed Mikania micrantha Kunth and its toxicity assessment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Kauser, S. Pal, I. Haq, M. Khwairakpam, Evaluation of rotary drum composting for the management of invasive weed Mikania micrantha Kunth and its toxicity assessment, Bioresour. Technol. 313 (2020) 123678.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of allelopathy on plant performance: a meta-analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhang, Y. Liu, L. Yuan, E. Weber, M. van Kleunen, Effect of allelopathy on plant performance: a meta-analysis, Ecol. Lett. 24 (2) (2021) 348–362.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Differential belowground allelopathic effects of leaf and root of Mikania micrantha

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Wu, H. Yu, S. Gao, Z. Huang, W. He, S. Miao, M. Dong, Differential belowground allelopathic effects of leaf and root of Mikania micrantha, Trees Struct. Funct. 23 (1) (2009) 11–17.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of the invasive plant Mikania micrantha HBK on soil nitrogen availability through allelopathy in South China

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Chen, S. Peng, G. Ni, Effects of the invasive plant Mikania micrantha HBK on soil nitrogen availability through allelopathy in South China, Biol. Invasions. 11(6) (2009) 1291–1299.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Lim, W. Shen, Z. Gao, Carbon quantum dots and their applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 44 (1) (2015) 362–381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Layered double hydroxide-carbon dot composite: high-performance adsorbent for removal of anionic organic dye

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhang, Q. Yao, C. Lu, Z. Li, W. Wang, Layered double hydroxide-carbon dot composite: high-performance adsorbent for removal of anionic organic dye, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 6 (22) (2014) 20225–20233.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plasma-enabled catalyst-free conversion of ethanol to hydrogen gas and carbon dots near room temperature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Zhou, R. Zhou, Y. Xian, Z. Fang, X. Lu, K. Bazaka, A. Bogaerts, K.K. Ostrikov, Plasma-enabled catalyst-free conversion of ethanol to hydrogen gas and carbon dots near room temperature, Chem. Eng. J. 382 (2020) 122745.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots modified graphene for full carbon-based potassium ion capacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Dong, Y. Song, Y. Fang, K. Zhu, K. Ye, Y. Gao, J. Yan, G. Wang, D. Cao, Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots modified graphene for full carbon-based potassium ion capacitors, Carbon 178 (2021) 1–9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as a dual sensor for the selective determination of D-penicillamine and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.B. Gunjal, A.H. Gore, V.M. Naik, S.P. Pawar, P.V. Anbhule, R.V. Shejwal, G.B. Kolekar, Carbon dots as a dual sensor for the selective determination of D-penicillamine and biological applications, Opt. Mater. 88 (2019) 134–142.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Lu, Y. Tang, J. Gao, Q. Wang, Concentrated solar irradiation protocols for the efficient synthesis of tri-color emissive carbon dots and photophysical studies, J. Mater. Chem. C 6 (47) (2018) 13013–13022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Signal transduction from small particles: sulfur nanodots featuring mercury sensing, cell entry mechanism and in vitro tracking performance
